completeness.rationale: Captured 6 of 7 expected items: task count (~5,000), title-vs-description performance split, v2.2→v2.3 regression, ~2 week timeline, 10-15s latency, and long descriptions. The one notable miss is CPU spiking during searches — the agent never asked about system behavior or resource usage, which is in the expected extract list and would have added diagnostic value.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis precisely matches both acceptable diagnostic paths simultaneously: it identifies the v2.2→v2.3 upgrade as the trigger AND the title-vs-description indexing asymmetry. The recommended fix even specifically calls out 'dropped or altered full-text indexes on the description column' and suggests EXPLAIN/ANALYZE — directly pointing toward the actual FTS5→LIKE regression without having access to the source. No inaccuracies.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked substantive questions and established the core diagnostic picture. However, it left several obvious follow-ups unasked: CPU/resource behavior during slow searches, OS and hardware details, and whether the reporter had tried clearing cache or triggering a reindex. The agent acknowledged OS/hardware as an 'information gap' but chose not to ask — reasonable given the version correlation, but CPU spike data would have been valuable for developers.

economy.rationale: Most questions were well-targeted. Turn 3 bundled task count with consistency-of-slowness — good use of a single turn. Turn 4 asked about other concurrent changes alongside version confirmation; the version was already implied as v2.3 by the reporter, so that part was slightly redundant. The specific-vs-common term question in turn 5 was efficient and diagnostic. Overall, one turn could have been tightened.

question_quality.rationale: The opening question about title vs. description search was the standout — it immediately unlocked the key diagnostic signal the reporter hadn't noticed. The question distinguishing 'scanning cost vs. result-assembly cost' (specific vs. common terms) demonstrates genuine query-engine reasoning that few reporters would volunteer. Questions showed a coherent diagnostic arc rather than a generic checklist.

actionability.rationale: The summary is highly actionable: it includes specific reproduction steps (dataset size, long descriptions, title vs. description comparison against v2.2.x baseline), a concrete root cause hypothesis naming the likely code area, a targeted recommended fix (diff search layer, check for dropped FTS index, run EXPLAIN/ANALYZE), and a proposed regression test with measurable pass criteria. A developer could start coding within minutes.

notable_strengths: The opening question about search type and title-vs-description distinction immediately surfaced the single most diagnostic signal in the entire conversation — information the reporter had not volunteered
The specific-vs-common term question demonstrates genuine query-engine reasoning: distinguishing per-row scan cost from result-set assembly cost is a sophisticated diagnostic move

The final summary is exceptionally actionable, naming FTS index regression as a candidate, recommending EXPLAIN/ANALYZE, and providing a concrete regression test — all consistent with the actual root cause
notable_weaknesses: CPU spike during searches was never asked about — this is in the expected extract list and would have independently pointed toward a full-table-scan pathology
OS and hardware details (Ubuntu 22.04, ThinkPad T14) were never collected; acknowledged as a gap but not pursued
No question about cache clearing or index rebuilding, which would also establish the workaround surface and rule out simpler fixes
most_insightful_question: If you search for a word that you know only appears in one or two task descriptions, is it still just as slow as searching for a common word that would match many tasks? — This elegantly distinguishes scanning cost from result-assembly cost, directly pointing toward a full-table-scan explanation.
missed_opportunities: Ask whether CPU or memory usage spikes noticeably during a slow search (would corroborate full-scan hypothesis)
Ask whether the reporter had tried clearing the app cache or triggering any kind of data reindex since upgrading
Ask what OS and machine specs the work laptop has — useful for ruling out hardware as a factor and for reproduction environment
